title: Idea about Reis seeming desire to die and her attempts at self sacrifice
display_title: Idea about Reis seeming desire to die and her attempts at self sacrifice
wikitext:
[]: Idea about Rei's seeming desire to die and her attempts at self-sacrifice             Quote Post by wissencraft » Fri Dec 02, 2022 8:14 pm One of the things that has always bugged me about Evangelion is Rei and how she can possibly be made to fit with the larger themes of the show. To me it seems somewhat bizarre when Rei expresses her desire to "return to nothingness" in Episode 25 and it is difficult for me to square that with her interactions with other characters and her actions in Episode 23. How human are her motivations? Does she desire freedom or death, or are the two the same to her? Recently I came up with an idea that might make the meaning of this wish for death more intelligible. I was reading a book by American Philosophy Robert Brandom about Hegel and came this section. It is an exposition of Hegel's Master-Slave dialectic, more specifically the life and death struggle for recognition. I will quote the section bit by bit and then explain how I believe it may be taken to apply to Rei. To say of an essentially self-conscious being that what it is for itself is an essential element of what it is in itself entails that an alteration in self conception carries with it an alteration in the self of which it is a conception. Essentially self-conscious creatures accordingly enjoy the possibility of a distinctive kind of self-transformation: making themselves be dif­ferent by taking themselves to be dif­ferent.

[]: Re: Idea about Rei's seeming desire to die and her attempts at self-sacrifice             Quote Post by nerv bae » Sat Dec 03, 2022 7:28 am If I cloned six samurai and told them to go out and do bushido stuff, including seppuku when necessary, then after a few years we might have an example population of: 1) One who'd committed seppuku properly, 2) Two who'd chickened out of seppuku and are now living in shame as animals, and 3) Three who're still chugging along -- maybe these guys are true samurai, maybe not, time will tell. Just because I can re-clone the first guy, who committed seppuku properly, doesn't mean that he didn't actually die to prove his samurai status, right? Everyone should agree that he was a superb samurai; now the second clone gets another chance to prove the same thing again. These guys in my example don't have continuity of soul or self between clonings, which is why I can make this argument (how could they, if there are six of them in the world at once?). But Rei does, in NGE? Like, in NGE serial Reis are all the same person somehow? Maybe someone can lorepost about Rei and Lilith's soul a bit to shed some light on this.

[]: Re: Idea about Rei's seeming desire to die and her attempts at self-sacrifice             Quote Post by Angel » Sun Dec 18, 2022 2:05 pm Rei didn't seek death. She sought Instrumentality and Gendo's plan for it. By the late episodes, however, she had turned against his plan and found more important things and people worth sacrificing herself for - but only if that sacrifice was necessary.

[]: Re: Idea about Rei's seeming desire to die and her attempts at self-sacrifice             Quote Post by Tumbling Down » Fri Apr 14, 2023 9:27 pm Angel wrote: Rei didn't seek death. She sought Instrumentality and Gendo's plan for it. By the late episodes, however, she had turned against his plan and found more important things and people worth sacrificing herself for - but only if that sacrifice was necessary.
